combined_devices.limitations: No published results from completed trial available in corpus; industry sole sponsor with no independent replication; no longitudinal sensitivity-to-change data; no MID/MICD; no regulatory clearance; algorithm proprietary and unpublished; validation domain scored Weak because results are unavailable despite well-designed concurrent-validity protocol

Feasibility study only (N=10); no clinical validation or correlation with disease severity/progression reported; high bias (very small sample, single site, no pre-registered analysis); no quantitative agreement metrics between EyePhone and VOG reported in abstract; no longitudinal data; no regulatory clearance; bias ceiling applies — High bias on sole paper caps at Moderate regardless of future metrics

combined_devices.regulatory_clearance: FDA Breakthrough Device Designation for monitoring ALS (not full clearance/approval)
combined_devices.limitations: Company involvement in FDA submission papers (bias Moderate); no completed Phase 3 RCT with articulatory precision as pre-specified primary endpoint found in corpus; MDC/MCID established against listener effort and perceptual ratings rather than a hard biomarker; ALSFRS-R speech item used as one anchor is itself a single ordinal item with known insensitivity.

measures_supported: Composite acoustic markers (articulation, phonation, prosody, resonance, timing)
combined_devices.limitations: No formal MDC/MCID reported in corpus; no FDA/CE clearance documented; multi-disease validation means ALS-specific longitudinal depth is limited (N=44 ALS in primary study); longitudinal data shows earlier detection than clinical scoring but psychometric properties (repeatability, MDC) not formally quantified; no trial endpoint use found.

combined_devices.device: Winterlight Labs Automated Speech Analysis Pipeline
measures_supported: 53 acoustic features spanning articulation, phonation, prosody, timing
combined_devices.limitations: No longitudinal sensitivity to change data in corpus (critical gap for endpoint use); no MDC/MCID; no regulatory clearance; explicitly named as Winterlight Labs pipeline but current commercial status uncertain (company acquired); AUROC=0.85 for ALS detection and 0.78 for early ALS are cross-sectional classification results, not endpoint-readiness evidence; strong analytical validation (Spearman ρ>0.70 vs lab-standard) but clinical validation limited to correlations.

Platform/manufacturer not identifiable from corpus (BLOCKING gap — cannot recommend a device that cannot be procured); single study (published + preprint = one dataset); despite strong responsiveness data (detects subclinical change), no MDC/MCID formally reported; video-based orofacial component adds multimodal richness but hardware requirements unclear; no regulatory status determinable.

Very small sample (11 bulbar-onset ALS patients); single study; no longitudinal data; no clinical anchor beyond syllable count accuracy (90.23%); purely analytical validation; academic prototype with no commercial path identified; narrow construct (DDK only).

combined_devices.device: Smartphone Tongue Lateralization Assessment (U-Net++ deep learning)
combined_devices.limitations: Single cross-sectional study (N=37 ALS, 20 controls); no longitudinal data; visual tongue task not validated against speech intelligibility outcome directly; novel approach with no replication; associated with dysarthria (p=0.003) but R2=0.43 in regression model indicates substantial unexplained variance; no commercial path identified.

Data collection tool only — the corpus describes a database (N=153) for research use, not a validated endpoint platform; no clinical validation against disease outcomes beyond F0 SD discriminating severity; no longitudinal data; no trial endpoint use; basic acoustic features without validated composite scoring; primarily a research resource rather than a clinical DHT.

Data collection platform only — no published results from the completed study (NCT04889898, N=200) available in corpus; no speech analysis algorithm validated; used alongside gold-standard measures (MBSS, SIT) suggesting it is an infrastructure tool for recording, not an analytical platform; academic research tool without commercial positioning for trials.

combined_devices.manufacturer: Multiple implementations (no single dominant commercial product named in corpus; protocol-defined systems used across HEALEY, COURAGE-ALS, Radcliff, Penn State studies)
combined_devices.limitations: No single named commercial product dominates; implementation varies across studies (coaching protocol, QC criteria, device hardware); COURAGE-ALS reported 65% of site personnel experienced technological problems and 82% completion at remote visits vs 96% in-clinic (PMID:40576049); concordance is well-correlated (rc=0.82) but not interchangeable — at-home VC progresses 28% faster than in-clinic, suggesting systematic bias requiring protocol-level specification of measurement setting (PMID:42366580); 72% of patients required caregiver help (PMID:34092157); MID/MICD for at-home VC not specifically established (gap).

Single validation study only; PEF validated against MS-PFT spirometer with excellent correlation (r=0.9644) but no repeated-measures longitudinal study demonstrating sensitivity to change over time; PEF correlated cross-sectionally with ALSFRS-R (rs=0.539) and predicted survival, but change scores not assessed; no use as a trial endpoint found in this corpus; single-center study from one geographic region.

combined_devices.manufacturer: unknown (described as affordable, portable digital device)
combined_devices.limitations: Single cross-sectional validation study; tested in clinical setting only (not home/remote); strong concordance with research spirometry (r=0.826, ρc=0.824, 95% CI 0.754-0.876, n=109) but no longitudinal sensitivity to change data; no trial endpoint use; cough peak flow is a clinically meaningful measure (CPF<270 L/min associated with ineffective airway clearance) but not established as a progression endpoint for ALS trials; portability suggests home use potential but not yet demonstrated remotely.

combined_devices.device: Smartphone Cough Sound App (sound-based CPF estimation)
measures_supported: Cough Peak Flow (CPF) estimated from cough acoustics
combined_devices.limitations: Single study with mixed NMD population (n=50; only 26 ALS); ICC=0.774 is adequate but below the 0.80 threshold typically required for substitution-grade agreement; high sensitivity (94.4%) and specificity (100%) for CPF<270 L/min threshold is promising for screening but not endpoint-grade; no longitudinal data; no trial use; authors explicitly state 'larger studies of more diverse patient cohorts are needed'; not yet demonstrated in unsupervised home setting; regulatory pathway unclear for software-as-medical-device acoustic algorithm.

combined_devices.manufacturer: Multiple (ResMed iVAPS, and other bilevel/volume-assured devices with data download capability)
combined_devices.limitations: Ventilator-derived parameters measure ventilatory support effectiveness, not independent respiratory muscle function — not a substitute for spirometry as a disease progression measure; primarily used for treatment optimization and adherence monitoring rather than as a standalone outcome; no formal validation against standard respiratory function measures as an equivalent endpoint; retrospective/observational designs dominate; mortality preceded by decreased NIV use rather than parameter changes (PMID:37753280), suggesting adherence itself may be more informative than derived respiratory metrics; COU limited to patients already on NIV (not the full ALS population).

combined_devices.manufacturer: Careportal (specific manufacturer not stated in corpus)
combined_devices.wear_location: Tablet device (home environment) with linked pulse oximeter
combined_devices.limitations: Single 24-week pilot study with n=13 only; high bias (small N, no comparator group, single site, uncontrolled design); described as having 'good feasibility and validity' but formal psychometric validation of the 26-item question set not reported in sufficient detail; 61 interventions made suggests clinical utility for proactive care but this is a process outcome not an endpoint measure; SpO2 maintenance during disease progression is a treatment-effectiveness finding, not a measure validation; would require substantially larger controlled studies to reach Tier 2.

combined_devices.manufacturer: Multiple (generic pulse oximeters; no specific brand named in corpus)
combined_devices.limitations: Used as a component in telemonitoring protocols and listed as an outcome in NCT07071935, but no standalone validation as a respiratory function progression endpoint in ALS in this corpus; SpO2 is a late marker of respiratory insufficiency in ALS (maintained until advanced disease due to compensation) and not sensitive to early decline; insufficient evidence in this corpus to score as an independent respiratory function DHT for trial endpoint use; ubiquitously available technology with mature sensor but lacking ALS-specific endpoint validation.

combined_devices.device: Microsoft Kinect 3D Depth Sensor (Reachable Workspace / RSA)
combined_devices.wear_location: Stationary camera (subject stands at fixed distance; no body-worn component)
measures_supported: Reachable workspace relative surface area (RSA) - total
Quadrant-specific RSA (upper lateral, upper medial, lower lateral, lower medial)
combined_devices.limitations: Device discontinued (Kinect consumer 2017, Azure Kinect 2023) — operationally unavailable for new trials without legacy stock or platform migration; all ALS validation uses small cohorts (n=10 ALS per study); clinic-only supervised assessment with no remote/home validation; no MID or MDC established for RSA in ALS; no RCT has used RSA as primary or secondary endpoint; single research group dominates ALS-specific publications (potential independence concern); methodology is transferable to successor depth sensors but cross-platform equivalence in ALS undemonstrated

combined_devices.device: Body-worn Inertial Measurement Units (dual IMU configuration, forehead + sternum)
combined_devices.manufacturer: unknown (generic research-grade IMUs; no specific commercial product named)
measures_supported: Head-neck angular velocity in three planes (flexion-extension, lateral bending, axial rotation)
Movement coupling (out-of-plane motion quantifying motor control impairment)
combined_devices.limitations: Single pilot study (n=13 ALS, n=13 controls); no specific commercial device identified — cannot be procured as a named product; no longitudinal data (cross-sectional discrimination only); no established gold-standard comparator correlation (authors note need for dynamometry/EMG validation); high inter-patient variability due to wide functional impairment range; no trial endpoint use; clinic-only supervised protocol; extension backward and lateral flexion most discriminating but effect sizes not provided with CI; explicitly described as 'pilot study' requiring further work

combined_devices.device: Dynamic Robotic Neck Brace (instrumented cervical motion + surface EMG)
measures_supported: Head-neck ROM in three planes (flexion-extension, lateral bending, axial rotation)
Surface EMG of neck muscles (sternocleidomastoid, extensors)
EMG onset timing and activation duration
Synchronized motion-EMG characterization of head drop
combined_devices.limitations: Custom research device with no identified commercial path; single study (n=11 ALS, n=10 HC); no longitudinal data; concurrent EMG adds mechanistic richness but increases operational complexity and limits scalability; positioned as characterization and clinic-complement tool rather than clinical endpoint; would require commercialization, standardization, and re-validation for trial deployment; correlation with ALSFRS-r and FVC demonstrated but no formal concurrent validity statistics reported beyond qualitative 'well correlated'

combined_gaps.description: NeuraLight completed trial (N=45) results not published or unavailable in corpus; ALSFRS-R correlation magnitude, comparison with validated eye-tracking system, and prediction model performance are all unknown
combined_gaps.action: Retrieve published results from NeuraLight trial or contact sponsor; this represents the single largest evidence source for oculomotor DHT validation in ALS

combined_gaps.description: No longitudinal sensitivity-to-change data exists for any oculomotor DHT in ALS; no repeated-measures design, no SEM/MDC, no MID/MICD established for any oculometric parameter
combined_gaps.action: Longitudinal study with repeated oculomotor measures in ALS patients over clinically meaningful interval (minimum 3-6 months) required before any endpoint use beyond exploratory

combined_gaps.description: No FDA/EMA guidance or precedent for oculomotor measures as clinical trial endpoints in ALS or any motor neuron disease; no device cleared for oculomotor assessment as a clinical endpoint instrument
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA Biomarker Qualification Program, EMA qualification opinions, and DDT guidance for any oculomotor/eye-tracking precedent in neurodegenerative disease; if absent, novel qualification pathway required and should be initiated early

combined_gaps.description: No measurement standardization: no consensus on which oculomotor parameters are most disease-relevant in ALS, no standard acquisition protocol, no algorithm specification, and no cross-platform equivalence testing
combined_gaps.action: Define candidate oculomotor biomarker panel grounded in ALS pathophysiology; establish minimum acquisition specifications (sampling rate, calibration procedure, stimulus protocol); test cross-platform agreement

combined_gaps.description: EyePhone developer identity, commercial status, and regulatory intent unknown from available corpus
combined_gaps.action: Identify EyePhone developer group, determine if commercially available or research-only, and assess regulatory pathway intent

combined_gaps.description: Corpus is extremely thin (2 records); high likelihood of additional oculomotor-in-ALS evidence not captured, including research-grade eye trackers (Tobii, SR Research EyeLink) used in ALS studies and other digital platforms
combined_gaps.action: Expanded search: eye tracking + ALS + disease progression; saccade + ALS; oculomotor + motor neuron disease; named eye-tracking platforms in ALS; and PI-name searches for known ALS eye-movement research groups

combined_gaps.description: No information on NeuraLight's data-sharing model, study-support infrastructure, or willingness to support pharma-sponsored trials
combined_gaps.action: Vendor engagement call with NeuraLight to assess: published results timeline, pharma partnership model, data access provisions, algorithm version control, and multi-site deployment capability

combined_gaps.description: No completed Phase 2/3 RCT in corpus uses any digital speech measure as a pre-specified primary or secondary endpoint. All evidence is observational/validation studies. The pathway from FDA Breakthrough Device Designation to actual trial endpoint qualification is undemonstrated.
combined_gaps.action: Identify ongoing or planned RCTs with digital speech primary/secondary endpoints; determine whether any sponsor has submitted an endpoint qualification package to FDA/EMA; query Aural Analytics and ki elements for trial partnership pipeline.

combined_gaps.description: MDC and MCID are established only for Aural Analytics articulatory precision. No other platform in the corpus reports these critical psychometric properties required for sample-size calculation and responder analysis in a pivotal trial.
combined_gaps.action: Request MDC/MCID data from ki elements for ki:SB-M; for Winterlight Labs, determine whether any longitudinal dataset exists from which these can be computed; for any device advancing to secondary endpoint, MDC<MCID must be demonstrated before protocol finalization.

combined_gaps.description: ki elements ki:SB-M and Winterlight Labs have no regulatory clearance or designation documented in the corpus. Regulatory status is unknown — could be CE-marked, could be research-use-only. Unclear if either has initiated a regulatory pathway for ALS endpoint use.
combined_gaps.action: Query both companies for regulatory strategy and current status; check FDA accessdata and EU EUDAMED for any submissions; determine if DiMe V3 validation (completed by ki elements per PMID:39108340) was submitted to any regulatory body.

combined_gaps.description: The multimodal remote monitoring platform (PMID:39126786) demonstrating strong longitudinal responsiveness in N=278 ALS participants cannot be identified from the corpus. The platform records audio and video for acoustic, orofacial, and linguistic biomarker extraction, but manufacturer and commercial availability are unknown.
combined_gaps.action: Identify the platform through author affiliations, funding disclosures, or direct inquiry; determine commercial availability and regulatory intent; if identifiable and commercially available, re-score at Tier 2.

combined_gaps.description: No head-to-head comparison between any two digital speech platforms exists in the corpus. Cannot determine whether articulatory precision (Aural Analytics), intelligibility score (ki elements), or the 53-feature approach (Winterlight) offers superior sensitivity to change or earlier detection in the same population.
combined_gaps.action: Design or identify a study comparing at least two platforms on the same ALS cohort with the same clinical anchors; alternatively, determine if existing datasets (e.g., Everything ALS recordings) have been analyzed by multiple pipelines.

combined_gaps.description: Winterlight Labs pipeline has no longitudinal sensitivity-to-change data in the corpus. Cross-sectional AUROC (0.85) and analytical validation (correlations >0.70) are strong but insufficient for endpoint use without demonstrated within-subject change detection.
combined_gaps.action: Determine whether longitudinal data exists from Winterlight/Cambridge Cognition; if not, this is a critical development gap requiring a dedicated longitudinal study before endpoint consideration.

combined_gaps.description: FDA Breakthrough Device Designation (Aural Analytics) is for 'monitoring' ALS, not for use as a registrational endpoint. The gap between BDD and endpoint qualification/acceptance by a division reviewing a therapeutic NDA/BLA is unquantified. No FDA guidance document specific to digital speech endpoints in ALS is identified in the corpus.
combined_gaps.action: Review FDA DDT qualification program and any relevant FDA guidance (Rare Disease, Digital Health Technologies, ALS-specific); query Aural Analytics for regulatory strategy beyond BDD; check if any Type C meeting minutes discuss digital speech endpoints.

combined_gaps.description: Several corpus papers (PMID:38837773, PMID:41396714, PMID:40365751, PMID:42159474) use smartphone apps for speech recording without naming the specific platform. These may represent additional evidence for Aural Analytics or another platform but cannot be attributed without full-text review of methods sections.
combined_gaps.action: Obtain full text of PMID:38837773, PMID:41396714, PMID:40365751, PMID:42159474 to identify the recording platform used; re-attribute evidence to specific devices accordingly.

combined_gaps.description: Commercial status of Winterlight Labs is uncertain — the company was acquired by Cambridge Cognition. Current product availability, pricing model, and willingness to support pharma trials under the new entity is unknown from the corpus.
combined_gaps.action: Contact Cambridge Cognition to confirm Winterlight platform availability for ALS trials, data access model, and regulatory intent.

combined_gaps.description: No MID/MICD (minimal important difference / minimal important clinical difference) established for at-home measured vital capacity in ALS. The 28% faster progression rate observed at home vs. in-clinic (PMID:42366580) implies the two settings may require different MID thresholds, but neither is formally derived in this corpus.
combined_gaps.action: Conduct anchor-based MID analysis linking at-home VC change to clinically meaningful milestones (NIV initiation, hospitalization, survival) using existing longitudinal datasets (HEALEY, Radcliff); establish whether in-clinic MID can be applied to at-home data or requires re-derivation.

combined_gaps.description: No specific named commercial spirometry device dominates the corpus or has been validated as THE reference home spirometry platform for ALS trials. Studies use different hardware (smartphone-connected, standalone mini-spirometers) without cross-device equivalence data.
combined_gaps.action: Identify and evaluate specific commercial spirometry products (e.g., Vitalograph, ndd EasyOne, Vyaire) used in the cited studies; obtain cross-device equivalence (ICC) data; determine whether protocol standardization (coaching, QC) matters more than hardware choice.

combined_gaps.description: At-home VC demonstrates systematic bias vs. in-clinic VC (28% faster decline, no systematic offset at baseline per PMID:42366580). The measurement setting introduces a protocol-dependent signal that is not purely noise. This has implications for trial design: cannot freely mix measurement settings without adjustment.
combined_gaps.action: Determine whether the 28% faster at-home decline reflects true physiology (unsupervised effort = more ecologically valid) or coaching/device artifact; specify a priori in trial SAPs whether at-home VC is the sole measurement modality or if conversion factor is needed for hybrid designs.

combined_gaps.description: No FDA qualification opinion, EMA qualification advice, or Biomarker Development Tool (BDT) designation for at-home spirometry as a drug development tool in ALS was identified in this corpus. VC is used as a trial endpoint by convention rather than formal regulatory endorsement of the remote modality.
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA DDT/BDT database and EMA qualification opinions for vital capacity in ALS; if absent, assess whether a Letter of Support or DDT qualification would de-risk primary endpoint use in a registrational trial.

combined_gaps.description: COURAGE-ALS (the largest hybrid DCT using remote FVC) reported substantial operational challenges: 65% of site personnel reported technological problems, FVC completion was 82% at remote visits vs. 96% in-clinic (PMID:40576049), and only 6% of SP reported no challenges in obtaining FVC remotely. Impact on data quality and missingness patterns for endpoint analysis is not fully characterized.
combined_gaps.action: Obtain COURAGE-ALS remote FVC data quality analysis (if published separately); characterize missing data patterns — determine if missingness is informative (sicker patients have more technical difficulties) which would bias ITT analysis.

combined_gaps.description: PEF measured by KOKA household peak flow meter shows excellent cross-sectional concordance (r=0.9644) and survival association, but no longitudinal sensitivity-to-change data exist. Without demonstrated responsiveness over time, PEF cannot be recommended as a longitudinal trial endpoint despite its convenience.
combined_gaps.action: Design or identify a longitudinal study measuring PEF at home at regular intervals in ALS patients to determine: rate of decline, SRM/effect size over clinically meaningful intervals, and concordance of PEF slope with FVC slope.

combined_gaps.description: Peak cough flow (both the digital meter and the smartphone app) lacks longitudinal validation in ALS. CPF is clinically meaningful (relates to airway protection and secretion clearance ability) but has no published change-score data, SEM, or responsiveness index in ALS over time.
combined_gaps.action: Identify whether ongoing studies (e.g., respiratory care RCTs in this corpus) are collecting serial PCF data that could be analyzed for responsiveness; if not, recommend adding PCF to an existing longitudinal ALS cohort.

combined_gaps.description: Smartphone cough sound app validated in only 26 ALS patients (within a 50-patient NMD cohort). ICC of 0.774 is below the 0.80 threshold typically required for substitution-grade measurement equivalence. Larger ALS-specific validation needed.
combined_gaps.action: Conduct ALS-specific validation study (n≥50 ALS) with test-retest reliability, and assess performance across disease stages (early vs. advanced bulbar involvement).

combined_gaps.description: The specific spirometry hardware used in the HEALEY Platform Trial and COURAGE-ALS hybrid DCT is not named in the abstracts available in this corpus. Full-text methods sections likely specify the commercial product, which is critical for device selection.
combined_gaps.action: Retrieve full-text methods sections of PMID:42366580 and the COURAGE-ALS protocol paper to identify specific spirometry hardware, software version, and coaching protocol used.

combined_gaps.description: No specific FDA 510(k)/De Novo clearance or CE marking for any home spirometry device with an intended use of 'monitoring respiratory decline in neuromuscular disease' or 'ALS trial endpoint' was identified. Standard spirometers are cleared for general pulmonary function testing but not with an ALS-specific indication.
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA accessdata for spirometry devices cleared for home use; determine whether existing general-purpose clearance is sufficient for trial use or whether an ALS-specific intended use would require supplemental submission.

combined_gaps.description: Primary validated ROM measurement platform (Microsoft Kinect) discontinued; no current-generation depth sensor or alternative hardware validated for RWS/RSA measurement in ALS
combined_gaps.action: Identify successor depth sensor platforms (Intel RealSense, Apple LiDAR, stereophotogrammetry systems, or marker-based motion capture) with potential for RWS implementation; determine whether any have published cross-platform equivalence with Kinect-derived RSA in any neuromuscular population; if none found, initiate platform migration and cross-validation study before any trial deployment

combined_gaps.description: No Minimal Important Difference (MID), Minimal Clinically Important Difference (MCID), or Minimal Detectable Change (MDC) established for RWS RSA in ALS; sample size planning for an RCT using RSA as endpoint is not currently possible
combined_gaps.action: Conduct anchor-based MID study linking RSA change magnitude to patient-meaningful functional milestones (e.g., loss of specific ADL such as feeding, grooming); compute SEM and MDC from test-retest reliability data in ALS cohort; estimate required trial N for powering RSA as secondary endpoint

combined_gaps.description: No RCT has used RWS RSA or any sensor-based ROM measure as a primary or secondary endpoint in ALS; gap between COA tool validation and trial deployment is complete
combined_gaps.action: Search ClinicalTrials.gov for any ongoing or completed ALS trials listing RWS, RSA, reachable workspace, or sensor-based ROM as outcome measure; if none found, consider proposing RSA as exploratory endpoint in next available ALS trial to generate interventional-context data

combined_gaps.description: All ALS-specific validation studies have small sample sizes (n=10-13 ALS patients); insufficient for subgroup analysis by onset type, establishing reliable decline trajectories, or characterizing inter-individual variability adequately
combined_gaps.action: Larger natural history cohort study (n>50 ALS, stratified by onset type and baseline severity) needed to characterize RSA decline trajectories, establish normative bands, and determine whether upper lateral quadrant RSA sensitivity generalizes across ALS phenotypes

combined_gaps.description: No FDA or EMA guidance identifies ROM, reachable workspace, or any sensor-based motion measure as a qualified or accepted endpoint in ALS; no Drug Development Tool qualification pathway documented
combined_gaps.action: Search FDA DDT qualification database and EMA qualification opinions for any ROM-based or motion-sensor-based digital measure in neuromuscular disease; identify closest precedent (e.g., stride velocity 95th percentile in DMD); assess whether RWS could enter LOI/qualification pathway based on existing evidence

combined_gaps.description: Head-neck ROM measurement in ALS lacks any identified commercial device; only generic unbranded research-grade IMUs used in pilot work; no standardized protocol, no named product, no path to trial deployment
combined_gaps.action: Evaluate commercial wearable IMU platforms (APDM/Movella Opal, Xsens DOT, BioSensics) for applicability to head-neck ROM protocol; determine if any have existing neuromuscular or cervical-motion validation that could support a bridging study in ALS

combined_gaps.description: No home-based or remote ROM assessment validated in ALS; all evidence is from supervised clinic-based sessions; incompatible with decentralized trial designs or continuous monitoring
combined_gaps.action: The narrative review references future remote monitoring via mobile sensing technologies but no validation exists; assess whether wrist-worn accelerometers or smartphone-based pose estimation could approximate RWS or limb ROM in unsupervised settings; feasibility study needed

combined_gaps.description: Corpus limited to 10 records; narrative review references 'more than a decade of experience in clinical studies' and validation in FSHD, DMD suggesting substantial additional primary literature not captured; universe likely undercounts devices and evidence
combined_gaps.action: Retrieve full reference list from PMID:41960733 to identify additional primary RWS validation studies in ALS and other NMD; search for post-Kinect RWS implementations (depth cameras, markerless motion capture, wearable IMU-based workspace estimation); search for commercial products implementing RWS methodology

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Oculomotor function in ALS encompasses quantitative eye movement parameters (saccades, smooth pursuit) that may be affected by disease progression involving frontal eye fields and brainstem circuits. Eye tracking is pursued as a potential passive, quantitative means for monitoring ALS disease status, with correlation to ALSFRS-R as the primary validation approach in current studies.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: ALS affects various eye movements; two studies directly assess oculometric parameters in ALS patients (NCT05407428, PMID:39015513)
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: No regulatory guidance, qualification opinion, or precedent for oculomotor measures as clinical trial endpoints in ALS identified in available corpus
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Commercial/near-commercial platforms exist (NeuraLight software platform, smartphone-based EyePhone) but validation remains at feasibility and early-correlation stage only (NCT05407428, PMID:39015513)

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Oculometric parameters (saccade metrics, smooth pursuit gain/accuracy)
measurement_spec.epoch: Not yet standardized; single recording sessions used in available studies
measurement_spec.algorithm: Not standardized; NeuraLight proprietary platform and EyePhone smartphone-based algorithms represent distinct unvalidated implementations

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Bulbar speech dysfunction in ALS encompasses progressive dysarthria resulting from upper and lower motor neuron degeneration affecting articulatory, phonatory, and respiratory musculature. It manifests as reduced articulatory precision, decreased speaking rate, increased pause frequency/duration, reduced intelligibility, and vocal quality changes. Up to 25% of people with ALS exhibit bulbar dysfunction at diagnosis, and it is a major determinant of communicative participation and quality of life.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Bulbar speech impairment is a cardinal feature of ALS affecting 25% at diagnosis with progressive decline; digital speech measures correlate strongly with disease-specific clinical scales (ALSFRS-R bulbar subscore, CNS-BFS) and detect subclinical change before patient-reported decline (PMID:38062079, PMID:39126786, PMID:38932502).
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: FDA Breakthrough Device Designation granted for an articulatory precision measure for monitoring ALS, following V3 framework validation; this establishes direct regulatory precedent for digital speech as an endpoint in ALS (PMID:38838248, PMID:38932502).
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Multiple validated platforms demonstrate smartphone-based remote speech collection with automated analysis achieving clinical-grade performance (R2=0.92 for severity prediction, r=0.9 correlation with perceptual ratings, AUROC=0.85 for ALS detection); presymptomatic detection demonstrated at AUC=0.81 (PMID:40341287, PMID:37309077, PMID:32138555, PMID:38311731).

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Articulatory precision (phoneme-level acoustic crispness score, continuous 0-100 scale)
measurement_spec.epoch: Per-recording session (standardized passage reading, ~60-99 words); daily or weekly collection demonstrated feasible
measurement_spec.algorithm: Phoneme-level acoustic analysis computing deviation from canonical pronunciation; secondary measures include speaking rate (words/min), percent pause time, intelligibility score (ASR-derived)
measurement_spec.valid_day_rule: Complete passage reading with identifiable speech signal; minimum 45-90 day calibration period for subject-specific prognostic modeling per PMID:37309077

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Respiratory function in ALS encompasses the progressive decline of respiratory muscle strength (diaphragm, intercostals, abdominals) leading to ventilatory insufficiency and ultimately respiratory failure, the primary cause of death. Key quantifiable constructs include vital capacity (FVC/SVC), maximal inspiratory/expiratory pressures (MIP/MEP), sniff nasal inspiratory pressure (SNIP), peak expiratory flow (PEF), and peak cough flow (PCF). Decline in FVC below 50% predicted is the standard threshold for NIV initiation and a well-established clinical milestone.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Respiratory failure is the primary cause of death in ALS; FVC/SVC decline is a core progression marker used in every major ALS trial in the corpus (PMID:40126464, PMID:42366580, NCT05193994, PMID:33792451).
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: Vital capacity is used as a secondary/combined endpoint in completed Phase 3 RCTs (COURAGE-ALS NCT04944784 per PMID:40126464; HEALEY Platform Trial per PMID:42366580; VITALITY-ALS per PMID:33792451) and is an established progression marker in regulatory guidance for ALS.
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Multiple validated home/remote spirometry implementations demonstrated concordance with in-clinic measurement (Lin's rc=0.82, n=233 per PMID:42366580) and feasibility in large-scale Phase 3 hybrid DCTs (n=486 per PMID:40126464).

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Vital Capacity (FVC or SVC) expressed as percent of predicted normal (%PN)
measurement_spec.epoch: Weekly to monthly home measurements; quarterly comparison to in-clinic gold standard
measurement_spec.algorithm: Maximum of 3 acceptable maneuvers per session; coached via live videoconference or autonomous with automated quality control; centrally reviewed for acceptability
measurement_spec.valid_day_rule: Minimum 1 acceptable maneuver per session meeting ATS/ERS repeatability criteria; sessions failing quality control excluded from analysis (per PMID:40630909 automated QC protocol)

combined_evidence.clinical_definition: Range of motion in ALS encompasses progressive reduction in active joint excursion due to motor neuron degeneration, manifesting as upper extremity reachable workspace contraction (quantified as 3D relative surface area), head-neck movement impairment (flexion-extension, lateral bending, axial rotation with reduced velocity and smoothness), and distal segmental ROM loss (wrist abduction-adduction, shoulder elevation). The COI is nascent as a DHT-measured clinical endpoint: longitudinal sensitivity is demonstrated for one sub-construct (reachable workspace RSA) but no MID/MDC is established and no RCT has deployed any sensor-based ROM measure as primary or secondary endpoint in ALS.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: Multiple studies demonstrate progressive ROM decline in ALS correlating with ALSFRS-r; longitudinal RSA decline of 19% in upper lateral quadrant over 1 year (p<0.01) (PMID:27813421); head movement velocity and smoothness significantly impaired versus controls (PMID:28068376); narrative review confirms RWS as valid COA with clinical utility in ALS (PMID:41960733)
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: No FDA or EMA guidance identifies ROM/RWS as a qualified or accepted endpoint in ALS; no Drug Development Tool qualification pathway documented in this corpus; RWS positioned as COA tool in peer-reviewed literature but regulatory acceptance remains undemonstrated (PMID:41960733)
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Primary validated measurement platform (Microsoft Kinect) discontinued; RWS/RSA methodology validated but requires platform migration to current-generation depth sensors; head-neck IMU measurement at single-pilot-study stage with no specific commercial device identified (PMID:28068376, PMID:41960733)

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Relative Surface Area (RSA) of 3D upper extremity reachable workspace, normalized by arm length; quadrant-specific RSA (upper lateral most sensitive to decline); secondary: head-neck angular velocity, movement smoothness, and movement coupling indices
measurement_spec.epoch: Single supervised clinic visit assessment session; longitudinal visits at quarterly or greater intervals (5 visits over 12 months validated)
measurement_spec.algorithm: 3D depth sensor reconstructs bilateral upper extremity workspace envelope during maximal reaching; surface area computed per quadrant and total, normalized to arm-length-squared; for head-neck: inertial sensor-derived angular kinematics across sagittal, coronal, and transverse planes during controlled single-plane movements (5 repetitions per plane)
measurement_spec.valid_day_rule: Not established in this corpus; studies used single-session supervised assessments with multiple repetitions averaged
